content:
# Judges Chapter 5 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_272.jpg, JDG_page_273.jpg

## The Song of Devorah and Baraq

1. And Devorah sang, and Baraq son of Avinoam, on that day, saying:

2. When locks of hair hung loose in Yisrael,
when the people offered themselves willingly —
bless YHWH!

3. Hear, O kings; give ear, O rulers!
I — to YHWH I will sing,
I will make music to YHWH, God of Yisrael.

4. YHWH, when you went out from Se'ir,
when you marched from the field of Edom,
the earth trembled, and the heavens dripped,
indeed the clouds dripped water.

5. The mountains flowed before YHWH —
this Sinai — before YHWH, God of Yisrael.

6. In the days of Shamgar son of Anath,
in the days of Ya'el, the highways ceased,
and travelers walked by crooked paths.

7. The villages ceased in Yisrael, they ceased —
until I arose, Devorah,
until I arose, a mother in Yisrael.

8. They chose new gods —
then war [was] in the gates.
Was a shield seen, or a spear,
among forty thousand in Yisrael?

9. My heart [is] to the commanders of Yisrael,
the ones offering themselves willingly among the people —
bless YHWH!

10. You who ride on white donkeys,
you who sit on rich carpets,
and you who walk on the road — declare [it]!

11. From the sound of those who divide [flocks] among the watering places,
there they recount the righteous acts of YHWH,
the righteous acts for his villages in Yisrael.
Then the people of YHWH went down to the gates.

12. Awake, awake, Devorah!
Awake, awake, utter a song!
Arise, Baraq, and take your captives, son of Avinoam!

13. Then a remnant went down to the nobles;
the people of YHWH went down for me among the mighty.

14. From Ephrayim, their root in Amaleq;
after you, Binyamin, among your peoples.
From Makhir, commanders went down,
and from Zevulun, those who bear the staff of the scribe.

15. And the princes of Yissakhar [were] with Devorah;
and Yissakhar, so [was] Baraq —
into the valley he was sent on his feet.
Among the divisions of Re'uven,
great [were] the searchings of heart.

16. Why did you sit among the sheepfolds,
to hear the pipings for the flocks?
Among the divisions of Re'uven,
great [were] the searchings of heart.

17. Gil'ad dwells beyond the Yarden,
and Dan — why does he sojourn by ships?
Asher sat at the shore of the seas,
and by his harbors he dwells.

18. Zevulun — a people who scorned their lives unto death,
and Naphtali, upon the heights of the field.

19. Kings came, they fought;
then the kings of Canaan fought
at Ta'anakh, by the waters of Megiddo —
plunder of silver they did not take.

20. From the heavens the stars fought;
from their courses they fought against Sisera.

21. The Wadi Qishon swept them away,
the ancient wadi, the Wadi Qishon.
March on, O my soul, with strength!

22. Then the hooves of horses pounded —
galloping, galloping of his mighty ones.

23. "Curse Meroz!" said the messenger of YHWH,
"Curse bitterly its inhabitants,
for they did not come to the help of YHWH,
to the help of YHWH among the mighty."

24. Most blessed among women [is] Ya'el,
the wife of Chever the Kenite;
among women in the tent, most blessed.

25. Water he asked — milk she gave;
in a lordly bowl she brought curds.

26. Her hand to the tent-peg she reached,
and her right hand to the workmen's hammer.
And she hammered Sisera, she crushed his head,
she shattered and pierced his temple.

27. Between her feet he sank, he fell, he lay;
between her feet he sank, he fell.
Where he sank — there he fell, destroyed.

28. Through the window she looked and cried out,
the mother of Sisera, through the lattice:
"Why is his chariot delayed in coming?
Why tarry the hoofbeats of his chariots?"

29. The wisest of her princesses answer her;
indeed, she returns her words to herself:

30. "Are they not finding, dividing the spoil? —
a womb, two wombs for the head of each man,
spoil of dyed cloth for Sisera,
spoil of dyed cloth, embroidered —
dyed cloth, two embroidered [pieces] for the neck of the spoil."

31. So may all your enemies perish, O YHWH!
But those who love him — as the sun going out in its might.

And the land had rest forty years.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.2 — "When locks of hair hung loose":** biphroa p'ra'ot b'Yisrael — one of the most debated phrases in the Song. p'ra'ot may mean "locks of hair" (warriors letting hair grow wild before battle) or "leaders leading" (from the root para', "to lead"). The ambiguity is ancient.

2. **v.7 — "a mother in Yisrael":** em b'Yisrael — Deborah calls herself "a mother in Israel," not "a judge" or "a prophetess." This self-identification is unique among the judges.

3. **v.26 — "she hammered... she crushed... she shattered... she pierced":** Four violent verbs in rapid succession — halmah, machaqah, machatsah, chalphah. The poetry intensifies with each verb. This is one of the most visceral lines in Hebrew poetry.

4. **v.27 — "Between her feet":** beyn ragleiha — the repetition is rhythmic and emphatic. The triple sequence "sank, fell, lay" (kara', naphal, shakhav) followed by "sank, fell" creates a decelerating rhythm mimicking the death itself.

5. **v.30 — "a womb, two wombs":** racham rachamatayim — literally "a womb, two wombs." A brutally euphemistic term for captured women as sexual spoil. The Hebrew is deliberately coarse, placed in the mouth of Sisera's mother and her princesses.

— EMET, AI Translator
